prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
* Deepak: In Sanskrit, the word "Brahmah" (No English equivalent for this word exists in English) means "beginning" and "Calm". It refers to the beginning of time and all the objects in space (like planets and their moons) are always in the calm part of the orbital mechanics. Deepak: September 19, 1964, I was born in a very small and not well-known country town in Western Europe called Kempten, in what is now the state of Germany. In my youth, I was practicing the ancient and traditional skills of watchmaking and I shared with my friends and my many young colleagues my skills in the production of mechanical watches, let them understand and prepare them to grasp the philosophy and principles of the right path of traditional watchmaking craftsmanship of centuries. Time has passed; I am about to leave my hometown and go to a quiet hill in the valley, where the mountain is surrounded by nature, peace and solitude, Where I am living in my old age and I am using for my work the skills and wisdom of traditional watchmaking.

In the above passage, “Brahmah” is a term used by some Sanskrit and English languages for the beginning and the absolute opposite of “Calm”. It is not translatable into English or any language that shares a time of inception before the invention of the practice of automatically translating it into a calm and peaceful state to reflect the beginning of time and the peaceful state of all objects in the space of the universe. Therefore, you can see in the English language the equivalent of the beginning and not the beginning of time, but not the end of time and not the peaceful state for the beginning of time, it must be the same as the beginning of time and peaceful state of all objects in the universe. So, I saw in my mind that immediately before the inception of time, all objects in the universe were in a state of perfect calm, peace and solitude, and in the deep valley of the traditional ancient craft of watchmaking There is a quiet and secluded place to be found, and the quiet and secluded place is not disturbed by noise and turmoil, but by silence and exquisite craftsmanship, where the craftsman performed his work tirelessly to express the joyful, peaceful and serene traditional skills, Qualification and work of his predecessors, endowed by the infinite wisdom of the time thread at the beginning of time.

In summary, Deepak provided a term that captures the essence and ideal state of the beginning, which in this case is a time of perfect calm, peace, and solitude for objects in the cosmos and traditional skills of watchmaking. Deepak's critics pointed out that this English equivalent is only applicable to the initial period of time and space objects, not the end of time and space objects. Deepak agreed, and provided a more concise term to describe the end of time and space objects as "ending a significant period of time and space objects". truce or serenity
